quick_facts.label: What NEST is for
quick_facts.value: NEST (National Entrance Screening Test) is the compulsory computer-based entrance test for the five-year Integrated MSc programmes at NISER Bhubaneswar and UM-DAE CEBS Mumbai.

quick_facts.label: 2026 eligibility baseline
quick_facts.value: For 2026 admission, applicants needed at least three of Biology, Chemistry, Mathematics and Physics in Classes XI–XII; Class XII passed in 2024 or 2025, or appearing in 2026; and 60% aggregate (55% for SC/ST/Divyangjan), as well as a NEST merit rank. There was no age limit.

quick_facts.label: 2026 exam structure
quick_facts.value: NEST 2026 was a three-hour CBT with four 60-mark subject sections—Biology, Chemistry, Mathematics and Physics—each containing 20 single-correct MCQs. Marking was +3 for correct, −1 for incorrect and 0 for unattempted.

quick_facts.label: Merit method
quick_facts.value: A candidate had to clear the section-wise minimum admissible score (SMAS) in at least three sections; when all four qualified, the total used the best three section scores. A category-specific minimum admissible percentile (MAP) was also required for a merit rank.

quick_facts.label: 2026 cycle status
quick_facts.value: The official 2026 portal lists the examination on June 6, 2026 and result announcement on June 24, 2026; its application process is closed. Dates, fees, intake and rules are cycle-specific and should be rechecked for a later NEST cycle.

sections.heading: Purpose and programmes accessed through NEST
paragraphs: NEST is the common admission examination for the five-year Integrated MSc programmes at NISER, Bhubaneswar, and UM-DAE CEBS, Mumbai. The official 2026 brochure describes the programmes as research-oriented basic-science education in Biology, Chemistry, Mathematics and Physics, with residential study, semester-and-credit curriculum, and early research exposure.

NISER is an off-campus centre of the Department of Atomic Energy’s Homi Bhabha National Institute, while UM-DAE CEBS was established jointly by the Department of Atomic Energy and the University of Mumbai; the programme affiliations differ accordingly. NEST is therefore an undergraduate-entry route aimed principally at research-oriented education in the basic sciences rather than a general engineering or medical entrance exam.
bullets: For the 2026–31 intake, the brochure listed 202 seats at NISER and 59 at UM-DAE CEBS, including supernumerary seats for eligible candidates from Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh. Intake is expressly proposed/cycle-specific and may change in subsequent years.

The 2026 brochure states that admitted students were eligible for a Department of Atomic Energy DISHA annual scholarship of ₹60,000 plus a ₹20,000 annual summer-internship grant. This financial support is dated 2026 information and applicants should verify the policy for their admission year.

sections.heading: Eligibility for the 2026 admission cycle
paragraphs: The published rules required a student to have studied at least three of the four basic-science subjects—Biology, Chemistry, Mathematics and Physics—in both Classes XI and XII. Candidates had to have passed Class XII from a recognised board in India in 2024 or 2025, or be appearing in 2026; candidates taking Class XII in 2026 had to meet the conditions when admitted.

The aggregate Class XII threshold was 60% or an equivalent grade for the unreserved category and 55% for SC, ST and Divyangjan candidates. The aggregate certificate had to be issued by a single board; where only letter grades were issued, the brochure required board documentation of their percentage equivalence. Admission also depended on securing a NEST 2026 merit-list rank and later original-document verification.
bullets: No upper age limit applied in the 2026 rules, provided the other admission conditions were met.

Reservation and certificate requirements followed Government of India rules; for example, OBC reservation required OBC-NCL status in the Central List, and category documentation was checked at admission.
These are 2026-cycle conditions, not a guarantee of eligibility in a future cycle.

sections.heading: Exam format and ranking
paragraphs: NEST 2026 was held as a computer-based test in one three-hour session. It had four subject sections—Biology, Chemistry, Mathematics and Physics—with 20 four-option, single-correct MCQs per section. Each section carried 60 marks, making 240 marks available across all four sections; the paper was offered in Hindi and English, with English prevailing if there was a discrepancy.

Candidates could attempt all four sections, but had to attempt at least three to enter the scoring process. For merit, they needed scores at or above the subject-specific SMAS in at least three sections and also had to meet the announced MAP. When a candidate qualified in all four subjects, the total was calculated from the best three eligible section scores; final rank was based on percentile.
bullets: NEST 2026 used a single-correct MCQ format; it is not safe to assume that question types or ranking mechanics will remain identical in a future brochure.
The official site maintains a question archive and, for the 2026 cycle, offered a CBT mock test for registered applicants.

sections.heading: How the 2026 application worked
paragraphs: The 2026 application was online only through the official NEST website. Applicants first registered with core personal and school details, then used the issued login credentials to complete the form, upload the required photograph and signature, select test-city preferences, and pay online. The official instructions stated that candidates did not need to send documents to the NEST office during the application process.

As dated 2026 context, the portal lists applications opening January 5, 2026 and closing April 8, 2026 after an extension; the examination took place June 6 and results were announced June 24. The portal now states that the application process is closed. Future applicants should use the next cycle’s official notice rather than reuse these deadlines.
bullets: The 2026 application fee was ₹1,400 for male/other UR and OBC applicants, and ₹700 for female applicants of any category and for UR-EWS, SC, ST and Divyangjan applicants. These fees are historical, cycle-specific context.
The official portal describes three phases: registration, logged-in form completion/submission, and helpdesk escalation for technical problems.
The 2026 brochure required a recent JPG/JPEG photograph and signature, each no larger than 80 KB; future upload specifications may differ.

sections.heading: Preparation approach grounded in the official syllabus
paragraphs: Start with the official syllabus rather than assuming that another science entrance syllabus is identical. For NEST 2026, the exam authority said the syllabus primarily followed the CBSE 2025–26 curriculum and NCERT Classes XI–XII books in Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry and Biology; some science knowledge up to Class X could also be needed.

A practical approach is to build subject coverage from the detailed official syllabus, then practise under the CBT constraints using the official mock test when available and the official previous-question archive. Since merit required qualifying in at least three subject sections, preparation should include at least three sciences and should not rely only on a single preferred subject.
bullets: Practise the 2026-style single-correct MCQ decision rule carefully because an incorrect response incurred −1 while an unattempted question incurred no deduction.
Use timed mixed-subject practice: the 2026 test allotted three hours for all four sections, and each section carried equal marks.
For a future attempt, confirm the then-current syllabus, mock-test availability, language rules, and pattern in that year’s official brochure.

sections.heading: What follows a NEST merit rank
paragraphs: A NEST merit rank does not itself complete admission: the 2026 rules made admission subject to the institutes’ merit-based process, satisfaction of academic eligibility, reservation rules where applicable, and verification of original documents at counselling/admission. The NEST portal directs candidates to the respective NISER and UM-DAE CEBS sites for institute merit-list and admission information.

The official brochure characterises the Integrated MSc pathway as research-focused and says that many graduates proceed to doctoral research in India or abroad, including at NISER or CEBS, and enter academia or industry primarily in research roles. That describes an institutional outcome profile, not a guaranteed job, placement, doctoral admission or salary result for an individual student.
bullets: For the 2026 cycle, only candidates who met both SMAS and MAP requirements received a merit rank.
The two institutes run the five-year Integrated MSc programmes in the four basic-science disciplines listed in the brochure.
No salary or placement figure is stated here because outcomes vary materially by discipline, further study, employer and year.
